name: THYROID CARCINOMA
definition: {(thyroid weight [g] x 80-120 µCi/g) π % uptake of 123I by 24 hours} x 100

demographics.Incidence: 25,000 new cancers per year in USA (2005); clinically silent cancers in up to 35%
demographics.Frequency: papillary carcinoma (in up to 40-90%) > medullary carcinoma (in up to
demographics.Path: encapsulated well-differentiated tumor without papillary elements; in 25% multifocal

distribution: color Doppler vascularity throughout instead of central hilar vessels only
discriminating_features: irregular ill-defined border without / disrupted halo

general: irregular ill-defined border without / disrupted halo
increased size with round bulging shape
cystic change (70% in metastatic papillary carcinoma)
color Doppler vascularity throughout instead of central hilar vessels only
PET: well-differentiated thyroid carcinomas are not FDG-avid
no change from pre-ablation: on suppression therapy
new foci (in up to 16%): consider therapy
decreased uptake: may be due to "stunning"
NUC: tumor usually concentrates radioiodine (even some purely papillary tumors) → 131I
usually concentrates pertechnetate, but fails to accumulate 123I
US: psammoma bodies = punctate echogenic foci less than 1 mm in size WITHOUT acoustic
purely solid / complex mass with areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, cystic degeneration
± invasion of carotid artery, internal jugular vein, larynx
XRAY: punctate / linear psammomatous calcifications at tumor periphery
CT: granular calcifications within fibrous stroma / amyloid masses (50%)
nodal spread to neck + mediastinum (in up to 50%) at time of presentation
distant metastases to liver, lung, bone (15-25%)
mass of low attenuation (no iodine concentration)

posttherapy scan (1 week after therapeutic dose) identifies more lesions than
clinical.Cx: radiation thyroiditis; radiation parotitis; GI symptoms (nausea, diarrhea); minimal
clinical.Rx: total thyroidectomy (← commonly multifocal); radioactive iodine ablation
